food_subgroup: North Indian combined meal: chickpea curry (chole) + deep-fried puffed wheat bread (bhature)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for chole bhature as a composite dish. Closest parent would be a legume-based curry dish category; no exact match available in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: North Indian chickpea curry with deep-fried leavened bread (closest parent match in FoodEx2)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0SAI used as closest parent for composite Indian curry-bread dish. No exact FoodEx2 code for chole bhature exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for chole bhature as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chole bhature consists of approximately 250g of chickpea curry (chole) served alongside 2 deep-fried puffed wheat breads (bhature) weighing approximately 120g, totalling ~370g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Chole Bhature — ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research - National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100g derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 Indian Food Composition Tables for chole bhature as a composite dish (weighted blend of chickpea curry and deep-fried wheat bread components). Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 where ICMR-NIN did not report specific values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chickpeas and Wheat-based Fried Bread — Composite Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chole bhature as a composite dish. GI estimated as weighted composite: chickpeas (kabuli chana) GI ~28 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021) contributing ~250g of the 370g plate, and deep-fried refined wheat bread (bhature/puri analogue) GI ~70 contributing ~120g. Weighted GI = (250 x 28 + 120 x 70) / 370 ≈ 41.6, adjusted upward to ~58 to account for the dominant glycaemic impact of the refined maida bhature starch and deep-fry oil coating effect on digestion rate. GL per serving = GI x available carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 58 x 72.1 / 100 ≈ 41.8. Chickpea GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (Foster-Powell et al.); bhature analogue (puri/deep-fried wheat bread) GI from Atkinson et al. 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~751mg per plate (203mg/100g x 3.7); moderate sodium load from spice blends and cooking salt
hypertension.guidance: One standard plate of chole bhature provides approximately 751mg sodium (~33% of the 2300mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should limit to half a plate or request reduced-salt preparation, and pair with potassium-rich accompaniments such as raw onion and cucumber raita to partially offset sodium impact.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~42 GL per plate) driven by 2 deep-fried refined wheat bhature; chickpea fibre partially moderates GI but cannot offset the dominant high-GL bhature component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat chole bhature as an occasional indulgence; if consuming, limit to 1 bhature (reducing carbohydrate by ~25g and GL by ~18 units), eat slowly with raw salad accompaniments, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the GL of ~42 for a full plate significantly exceeds the recommended per-meal GL target of ≤20.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil absorption in bhature (~16g absorbed oil per 2 bhature) raises total fat to ~31g per plate; saturated fat is relatively low (~4.4g) as vegetable oil is used, but total caloric density (~740 kcal/plate) and refined carbohydrate load are concerns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high caloric density (~740 kcal), moderate sodium (~751mg), and significant refined carbohydrate from bhature are the primary concerns; the chickpea component provides beneficial soluble fibre (~10g per plate) that supports LDL reduction, but the dish should be consumed occasionally and portion-controlled to 1 bhature with a full serving of chole to maximise the cardioprotective chickpea fibre benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chickpea prebiotic fibre (~10g per plate) supports gut microbiome; non-haem iron (~5.5mg) with vitamin C from tomato enhances absorption; vegetable oil used for frying is PUFA-rich with lower SFA than butter-fried alternatives
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chole bhature is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the chickpea component provides prebiotic fibre supporting gut-inflammation modulation, and the tomato-based gravy provides vitamin C to enhance non-haem iron absorption from chickpeas.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chole Bhature is a classic Punjabi North Indian dish consisting of two components served together as one plate: 'chole' (spiced chickpea curry, ~250g) and 'bhature' (2 large deep-fried puffed leavened wheat breads, ~120g), totalling approximately 370g per serving and ~740 kcal. The chole is a dark, intensely spiced chickpea curry slow-cooked with tomato, onion, ginger, garlic, garam masala, amchur (dried mango powder), anardana (pomegranate seed powder), and tea leaves for colour. The bhature are made from all-purpose flour (maida) leavened with yogurt and baking powder, then deep-fried until they puff into golden balloons. Texture: chole is thick, hearty, and deeply savoury; bhature are crisp on the outside, soft and airy inside, with a rich oily finish from deep-frying. Nutritionally per 100g: 200 kcal, 5.9g protein, 8.4g fat (1.2g saturated), 24.3g carbohydrate, 2.7g dietary fibre, 203mg sodium. Per plate: ~740 kcal, ~22g protein, ~31g fat, ~90g carbohydrate, ~10g dietary fibre, ~751mg sodium. GI ~58 (medium), GL ~42 (high) — the bhature refined wheat component dominates the glycaemic load. Chickpeas contribute significant prebiotic fibre and non-haem iron. Clinically: high GL makes this unsuitable for regular consumption by T2DM patients; moderate sodium is manageable for most hypertensive patients with portion control. A hallmark of North Indian street food and festive Sunday brunch culture, widely available at Indian restaurants in Singapore's Little India (Ananda Bhavan, Saravana Bhavan). Source: ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017.

chickpea curry with fried bread nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017, the primary approved source for Indian dishes. GI estimated as a defensible weighted composite from University of Sydney GI Database values for chickpeas and analogous deep-fried wheat bread (puri), with clear methodology documented. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9. Overall confidence is high given the strong primary source alignment for the dominant nutrient values.
notes: vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: chole bhature is a vegetarian dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN 2017 for this dish type. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: no vitamin D-containing ingredients (fatty fish, fortified dairy, egg yolk) are present in this dish; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN 2017. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: vegetable oil used for deep-frying bhature does not contribute significant industrially-produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN 2017. FoodEx2 code A0SAI used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code for chole bhature as a composite dish exists. GI is an analogous estimate (weighted composite method) as no direct GI measurement for chole bhature as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per plate (~42) is high and clinically significant for T2DM management. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by ICMR-NIN for this dish; set to -1. Water content not reported by source; set to -1. Copper and manganese sourced from enriched_json (confidence 0.9); vitamin K sourced from enriched_json (confidence 0.9) as ICMR-NIN 2017 does not routinely report these micronutrients for composite dishes.

Documented GI estimation methodology with University of Sydney GI Database as approved source.
